title: Fractions, Decimals & Ratios
blurb: Master fractions, decimals, percents, and ratios — the backbone of GRE Quant arithmetic.
theory:
What Is a Fraction?

A fraction a/b represents a parts out of b equal pieces. The top number is the numerator (a), the bottom is the denominator (b). The denominator can never be 0. A fraction is just division: 3/4 means 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75.

A fraction equals 1 when numerator and denominator match (5/5 = 1), is less than 1 when the top is smaller (proper fraction, like 2/3), and is greater than 1 when the top is bigger (improper fraction, like 7/4).

Equivalent Fractions & Simplifying

Multiplying or dividing top AND bottom by the same nonzero number gives an equivalent fraction: 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 50/100. To simplify (reduce), divide top and bottom by their greatest common factor. Example: 12/18 → divide both by 6 → 2/3. Always reduce your final answer.

Adding & Subtracting Fractions

You can only add or subtract fractions that share a common denominator. Steps:

  • Find a common denominator (the least common multiple of the denominators works best).
  • Rewrite each fraction with that denominator.
  • Add or subtract the numerators; keep the denominator.
  • Simplify.

Example: 1/4 + 2/3. LCM of 4 and 3 is 12. So 1/4 = 3/12 and 2/3 = 8/12. Sum = 11/12.

Quick trick (bowtie / cross method): a/b + c/d = (a×d + c×b)/(b×d). For 1/4 + 2/3 = (1×3 + 2×4)/(4×3) = (3+8)/12 = 11/12.

Multiplying & Dividing Fractions

Multiply straight across: (a/b)×(c/d) = (a×c)/(b×d). No common denominator needed. Cancel common factors before multiplying to keep numbers small: (3/4)×(8/9) = (3×8)/(4×9) → cancel 3 with 9 and 4 with 8 → (1×2)/(1×3) = 2/3.

Divide by multiplying by the reciprocal (flip the second fraction): (a/b) ÷ (c/d) = (a/b)×(d/c). Example: (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3)×(5/4) = 10/12 = 5/6.

Mixed Numbers & Improper Fractions

A mixed number like 2 3/4 means 2 + 3/4. Convert to improper: multiply whole by denominator, add numerator, keep denominator: 2 3/4 = (2×4 + 3)/4 = 11/4. Convert back by dividing: 11/4 = 2 remainder 3 = 2 3/4. Always convert mixed numbers to improper before multiplying or dividing.

Comparing Fractions

Three reliable methods:

  • Cross-multiply: To compare a/b and c/d (positive denominators), compare a×d with c×b. The larger product sits over the larger fraction. For 3/7 vs 4/9: 3×9 = 27, 4×7 = 28; since 28 > 27, 4/9 > 3/7.
  • Common denominator: Rewrite both with the same bottom and compare numerators.
  • Benchmarks: Compare each to 1/2 or 1. 5/9 > 1/2 (since 5 > half of 9), while 4/9 < 1/2.

Fractions ↔ Decimals ↔ Percents

These are three languages for the same idea.

FractionDecimalPercent
1/20.550%
1/40.2525%
1/50.220%
1/80.12512.5%
1/30.333…33.3%
3/40.7575%
  • Fraction → decimal: divide top by bottom.
  • Decimal → percent: multiply by 100 (move decimal 2 places right). 0.45 = 45%.
  • Percent → decimal: divide by 100 (move 2 places left). 8% = 0.08.
  • Percent → fraction: put over 100 and simplify. 40% = 40/100 = 2/5.

Decimal Place Value & Operations

Each place right of the decimal point is a tenth, hundredth, thousandth: 0.376 = 3 tenths + 7 hundredths + 6 thousandths. When adding/subtracting decimals, line up the decimal points. When multiplying, multiply as whole numbers, then place the decimal so the answer has as many decimal places as both factors combined: 0.3 × 0.02 = 0.006 (1 + 2 = 3 places). When dividing, shift both decimals right until the divisor is a whole number: 1.2 ÷ 0.4 = 12 ÷ 4 = 3.

Ratios

A ratio compares quantities and can be written 3:2, 3 to 2, or 3/2. Ratios show relative size, not actual amounts — 3:2 could mean 3 and 2, or 30 and 20, or 300 and 200. The shared scaling number is called the multiplier (or part value).

Part-to-part vs part-to-whole: If a class has boys to girls of 3:2, that is part-to-part. The total has 3 + 2 = 5 parts, so boys are 3/5 of the whole (part-to-whole) and girls are 2/5. Always check which the question wants.

Scaling Ratios & Finding Totals

Use the multiplier x. If boys:girls = 3:2 and there are 30 boys, then 3x = 30, so x = 10, and girls = 2x = 20, total = 5x = 50. Because the total is split into whole parts, the actual total must be a multiple of the sum of the ratio terms.

Proportions

A proportion sets two ratios equal: a/b = c/d. Solve by cross-multiplying: a×d = b×c. Example: if 4 pens cost $6, how much do 10 pens cost? 4/6 = 10/x → 4x = 60 → x = 15. Keep units consistent on each side (pens with pens, dollars with dollars).

Unit Conversion & Dimensional Analysis

Multiply by conversion factors written as fractions equal to 1, arranged so unwanted units cancel. To convert 90 km/hr to m/s: 90 km/hr × (1000 m / 1 km) × (1 hr / 3600 s) = 90000/3600 m/s = 25 m/s. The km and hr units cancel, leaving m/s. Track units like algebra — whatever appears on top and bottom cancels.
whatEtsTests:
• Translating fluently among fractions, decimals, and percents under time pressure.
  • Comparing fractions quickly via cross-multiplication and 1/2 benchmarks (heavy in Quantitative Comparison).
  • Part-to-part vs part-to-whole ratio distinctions, especially in word problems.
  • Scaling ratios and recognizing that real totals must be multiples of the ratio-part sum.
  • Proportions in rate, cost, recipe, and map/scale contexts.
  • Multi-step fraction arithmetic with mixed numbers and nested operations.
  • Decimal place-value precision in multiplication and division.
  • Unit conversion / dimensional analysis embedded in rate and measurement problems.
tips: To compare two fractions fast, cross-multiply upward: the product over each numerator tells you which fraction is bigger — no common denominator needed.
Memorize the common fraction-decimal-percent conversions (1/8=12.5%, 1/6≈16.7%, 1/3≈33.3%, 3/8=37.5%) so you never compute them mid-problem.
Cancel common factors BEFORE multiplying fractions — it keeps numbers tiny and avoids reducing a giant product later.
Benchmark against 1/2: if the numerator is more than half the denominator, the fraction exceeds 1/2. Great for QC and estimation.
The actual total in a ratio problem is always a multiple of the sum of the ratio parts — use this to eliminate answer choices instantly.
To divide by a fraction, multiply by its reciprocal; 'dividing by 1/2 is the same as doubling' often saves a full computation.
traps: Adding numerators AND denominators straight across (1/2 + 1/3 ≠ 2/5). You must use a common denominator.
Confusing part-to-part with part-to-whole — a 3:2 ratio makes a part 3/5 of the whole, not 3/2.
Forgetting to convert a mixed number to an improper fraction before multiplying or dividing.
Misplacing the decimal point in multiplication — count total decimal places in BOTH factors.
Assuming a ratio gives actual quantities; 3:2 is relative and needs a multiplier or a given total to find real values.

examples.steps:
1. Find the LCM of 6 and 8, which is 24.
  2. Rewrite: 5/6 = 20/24 and 3/8 = 9/24.
  3. Subtract numerators: 20/24 − 9/24 = 11/24.
  4. 11 and 24 share no common factor, so 11/24 is already lowest terms.

1. Cross-multiply: 7×9 = 63 and 5×12 = 60.
  2. The product 63 sits over 7/12, and 60 over 5/9.
  3. Since 63 > 60, the fraction 7/12 is greater.
examples.shortcut: Both exceed 1/2 (half of 12 is 6, half of 9 is 4.5). 7/12 ≈ 0.583 vs 5/9 ≈ 0.556, so 7/12 wins.

examples.q: In a parking lot the ratio of cars to trucks is 5:3. If there are 40 vehicles total, how many are trucks?
Convert 90 kilometers per hour to meters per second.
examples.steps:
1. Write conversions as fractions: 1 km = 1000 m, 1 hr = 3600 s.
  2. 90 km/hr × (1000 m / 1 km) × (1 hr / 3600 s).
  3. The km and hr units cancel, leaving 90×1000/3600 m/s.
  4. 90000/3600 = 25 m/s.
examples.shortcut: Divide km/hr by 3.6 to get m/s: 90 ÷ 3.6 = 25 m/s.

questions.q: The ratio of red to blue marbles is 2:5. If there are 14 red marbles, how many blue marbles are there?
questions.explain: 2 parts = 14, so each part = 7. Blue = 5 parts × 7 = 35.

questions.q: What is (2/5) × (3/4)? Enter your answer as a decimal.
A recipe uses sugar to flour in a 2:5 ratio. If a baker uses 7 1/2 cups of flour, how many cups of sugar are needed?
questions.explain: Set up proportion: sugar/flour = 2/5 = s/7.5. Cross-multiply: 5s = 15, so s = 3 cups.
Cross-multiply: 4×9 = 36 over 4/7, and 5×7 = 35 over 5/9. Since 36 > 35, 4/7 > 5/9. Quantity A is greater.

questions.q: Which of the following fractions are greater than 1/2? Select all that apply.
questions.explain: Compare each numerator to half its denominator. 5/9: half of 9 is 4.5, 5 > 4.5 ✓. 7/15: half is 7.5, 7 < 7.5 ✗. 9/16: half is 8, 9 > 8 ✓. 3/7: half is 3.5, 3 < 3.5 ✗. 11/20: half is 10, 11 > 10 ✓. So 5/9, 9/16, and 11/20.

questions.q: A car travels at 72 kilometers per hour. What is this speed in meters per second?
In a bag of red and green marbles, the ratio of red to green is 4:7.
questions.info: In a bag of red and green marbles, the ratio of red to green is 4:7.
questions.qa: The fraction of marbles that are red
questions.explain: Red is part-to-part 4:7, but the fraction of the WHOLE that is red is 4/(4+7) = 4/11. Compare 4/11 with 4/7: same numerator, larger denominator (11) means smaller value, so 4/11 < 4/7. Quantity B is greater. The trap is confusing part-to-part with part-to-whole.

questions.q: The ratio of a to b is 3:4 and the ratio of b to c is 6:5. What is the ratio of a to c in lowest terms?
questions.explain: Make b common. a:b = 3:4 and b:c = 6:5. Scale a:b to b=12 → a:b = 9:12. Scale b:c to b=12 → b:c = 12:10. So a:b:c = 9:12:10, giving a:c = 9:10.

questions.q: After spending 1/3 of his money on books and 2/5 of the remainder on food, John has $36 left. How much money did he start with?
questions.explain: Let the start be M. After books, 2/3 M remains. He spends 2/5 of that on food, leaving 3/5 of 2/3 M = (3/5)×(2/3)M = 2/5 M. So 2/5 M = 36, giving M = 36 × 5/2 = $90.
